votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq specification's logic, including the bidirectional implication for the result being -1 and the conditions for the result being the maximum even number in the range.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking the equivalence for result == -1 and verifying that result is the maximum even number in the range when result != -1.

The Python code correctly implements the logical conditions of the Coq specification. It verifies that result is -1 if and only if all numbers in the range are odd, and otherwise verifies that result is the maximum even number in the range.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks the biconditional (result = -1 iff all numbers in range are odd), and when result != -1, it verifies that result is even, within range [x,y], and is the maximum even number in that range. The logic matches the Coq spec for all concrete inputs.

Implements the bi-implication for result = -1 exactly, checks evenness and in-range, and enforces result as the maximal even in [x,y]. Handles empty ranges via vacuous truth; parity checks match Z parity.

The Python code enforces the same two conditions: result == -1 iff all integers in [x,y] are odd (with empty ranges handled vacuously), and if result != -1 it is an even number within [x,y] that is at least as large as every even in the range. Parity checks and range handling match Coq’s Z semantics for all integers.

Checks result = -1 iff all integers in [x,y] are odd (including empty range), otherwise ensures result is an even in [x,y] and ≥ every even in the range (the max even). Parity and range handling align with Coq’s Z semantics.
